title: Sources of Magnetic Fields and Magnetizing Current
narrationScript: You know the two field directions. Now we look at where the field comes from in the first place, and what kind of current drives it, because those two choices set how deep you can see and how well the particles move. Start with the oldest source, the permanent magnet. A permanent magnet yoke needs no power supply at all, no cables, no arcing, nothing to spark. That makes it the tool of choice in a few specific places, inside tanks with flammable vapors, on rigs where no power is allowed, underwater work, anywhere a spark or a power cord is the bigger hazard. But the limitations are serious. You cannot adjust the field strength, the field falls off as the magnet ages or chips, a strong magnet is genuinely hard to pull off a part and can pinch you on the way, and particles and steel dust cling to the poles constantly. You also cannot switch it off to demagnetize. So permanent magnets are a niche tool, legal where your procedure allows them, and always checked with a lifting test before use. Electromagnets fix those problems by making the field with current through a winding. The electromagnetic yoke is the most common piece of MT equipment in the field, a U-shaped core with a coil in the handle. Energize it, and flux flows from pole to pole through the part between the legs, a local longitudinal field you can point anywhere just by rotating the yoke. Yokes are proven by a lifting test, an AC yoke should lift at least ten pounds at the pole spacing you use, and a DC yoke at least forty pounds. Those are the classic check values in wide use, your procedure states yours. Bench units scale the same idea up, contact heads for circular shots, a built-in coil for longitudinal shots, and a tap switch or control to set amperage. Now the current itself. Alternating current reverses direction a hundred and twenty times a second here in North America, twice every sixty-hertz cycle, and that constant reversal pushes the magnetic field out to the surface of the part. We call that the skin effect. The result is a field concentrated in the outer skin, which makes AC superb for surface cracks and nearly blind to anything deeper. The reversal also shakes the particles, giving them mobility, they dance across the surface and gather into leakage fields instead of sticking where they land. So for surface fatigue cracks on a weld or a machined part, AC is the sharp tool. One more gift, an AC field that steps down in strength leaves the part essentially demagnetized, so AC equipment demagnetizes easily. Direct current flows one way all the time, and it penetrates. Full-wave rectified DC, what bench units produce from three-phase power, gives the deepest, smoothest field and the best shot at subsurface discontinuities a little below the surface. The trade is that particles sit quieter, and a DC residual field is stubborn, you cannot shake it out with a simple step-down, deep residual fields need a reversing and decreasing DC demag cycle. In between sits half-wave rectified DC, made by clipping one half of the AC cycle. What is left is a pulsating one-directional current, the pulses give particle mobility almost like AC, while the one-way average gives real penetration. That combination makes half-wave DC with dry powder the classic choice for rough welds and castings where slightly subsurface flaws matter. Pure battery DC is mostly history now, but you will still hear old hands call rectified current DC, and the books often say it too. So the choice runs like this. Surface cracks on machined or welded steel, AC, best mobility, best surface sensitivity, easy demag. Possible slightly subsurface flaws, dry powder field work, half-wave DC. Maximum penetration on a bench with wet suspension, full-wave DC. And if no power or no sparks are permitted, the permanent magnet earns its keep. Whatever the source, respect the electricity. Magnetizing equipment pushes hundreds or thousands of amperes at low voltage, and the arc risk lives at every contact point. Keep contacts clean and firm, never break a live circuit, and treat damaged cables as out of service. The current type you choose decides what you can find. Choose it on purpose, then prove the field like always.
imagePrompt: A welder-turned-inspector holds an electromagnetic yoke with its two articulated legs planted across a structural steel weld, dry red powder dusted between the poles in an industrial fabrication shop.

sections.heading: Permanent Magnets: Field Without Power
sections.body:
The simplest field source is a **permanent magnet yoke** - two poles on a hard-magnet core, no power supply, no cables, no current. Flux flows from pole to pole through the part between them, giving a local longitudinal field just like an electromagnetic yoke.

That independence from power is the whole case for them: inside tanks or vessels with **flammable atmospheres**, on sites where hot work and powered equipment are restricted, and underwater, a permanent magnet cannot arc and cannot spark. 

The limitations are just as real:

- **No control** - field strength cannot be adjusted, switched off, or stepped down for demagnetization.
- **Strength degrades** with age, chipping, and abuse, so a **lifting-power check before use** is mandatory.
- A magnet strong enough to inspect with is **hard to place and remove** - it slams onto the part and fights you coming off.
- Particles, chips, and steel dust **cling to the poles** continuously, contaminating the work area.

Most procedures treat permanent magnets as a special-permission tool: allowed where written in, verified by lift test, and otherwise left in the box.

sections.heading: Electromagnets: Yokes and Bench Units
sections.body:
Wind a coil around an iron core and you have an **electromagnet** - a field you can switch on, adjust, and switch off. The **electromagnetic yoke** is the most common MT tool in field work: a U-shaped laminated core with the energizing coil in the handle, often with articulated legs to fit curved surfaces. Energized, it drives flux **pole to pole through the part between its legs** - a local longitudinal field aimed simply by rotating the yoke. Two placements roughly 90 degrees apart cover both flaw orientation families in the area between the poles.

Yoke performance is proven by the **lifting test** at the pole spacing to be used: an **AC yoke lifts at least 10 pounds**; a **DC (or permanent magnet) yoke lifts at least 40 pounds** - classic check values used by practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V Article 7; your procedure states the requirement and the check interval.

**Bench (wet horizontal) units** combine the sources you already know: contact heads for direct circular shots, a central conductor bar for indirect circular shots, and a built-in coil for longitudinal shots, with amperage control and a particle bath in one machine - the production workhorse for machined parts.

sections.caption: Three field sources side by side: a permanent magnet yoke, an electromagnetic yoke with its lifting-test values, and a bench unit combining heads and coil.

sections.heading: Alternating Current: Surface Field, Lively Particles
sections.body:
**Alternating current** reverses direction twice per cycle of the line frequency - 120 times per second on 60 Hz power in North America. That continuous reversal confines the magnetic field to the outer layer of the part: the **skin effect**. The consequences define AC's character:

- The field concentrates **at and just below the surface**, making AC the most sensitive choice for **surface-breaking cracks** - and essentially blind to subsurface discontinuities.
- The pulsating field **vibrates the particles**, giving them outstanding **mobility**: they migrate across the surface and assemble into leakage fields instead of sticking where they land.
- Because the field constantly reverses, **stepping AC down to zero leaves the part essentially demagnetized** - AC equipment doubles as the standard demagnetization source.

For surface fatigue cracks on welds, machined parts, and in-service components, AC is the default in many procedures. Its one firm boundary: if the flaw of interest may be **below the surface**, AC is the wrong current.

sections.heading: Direct Current: Penetration
sections.body:
**Direct current** flows one way continuously, so the field it produces fills much more of the part's cross-section - **depth of penetration** is DC's reason for existing.

- **Full-wave rectified DC (FWDC)** - produced by rectifying (usually three-phase) AC - gives the deepest, smoothest field and the best capability for discontinuities **slightly below the surface**. It is the standard high-output current on bench units running wet suspension. Trade-offs: particles are less mobile than under AC, and a DC residual field is stubborn - deep residual fields need a **reversing and step-down DC** demagnetization cycle rather than a simple AC pass.
- **Half-wave rectified DC (HWDC)** clips one half of the AC cycle, leaving a **pulsating one-directional current**. The pulses shake the particles almost like AC while the one-way average penetrates like DC - the combination that makes **HWDC with dry powder** the classic field choice for rough welds and castings where near-subsurface flaws matter.
- **Battery (straight) DC** is largely historical, but the term DC in older texts and procedures usually means rectified current today.

Remember the pairing logic: the current type sets **where the field lives** in the part; the particle system (next module) sets how you see it.

sections.caption: Current waveforms used in MT - AC, half-wave DC, and full-wave DC - with their relative depth of field penetration.
Magnetizing current comparison - typical characteristics; the written procedure governs selection.
rows: Stubborn residual; needs reversing DC demag

sections.title: Hundreds of amperes, zero forgiveness
sections.body: MT power sources push **hundreds to thousands of amperes** at low voltage. The hazard concentrates at contact points: keep contacts clean and clamped firm, **never make or break a connection with current flowing**, inspect cables and lugs each shift, and tag out damaged equipment. Arc flash at a head, prod, or loose lug can burn the part - and you.

sections.heading: Choosing the Source and Current on Purpose
sections.body:
Pull the lesson together as a selection habit:

- **Surface cracks on machined or welded steel** - AC: skin-effect sensitivity, best particle mobility, easiest demagnetization.
- **Possible slightly subsurface flaws, field conditions, dry powder** - HWDC: penetration plus pulsation.
- **Maximum penetration on the bench with wet suspension** - FWDC.
- **No power or no spark permitted** - permanent magnet yoke, lift-tested, where the procedure allows.

Then prove the result the same way as always: lifting test for yokes (about 10 lb AC, about 40 lb DC at the working pole spacing), QQI shims or a pie gauge on the part, a Hall-effect gaussmeter where the procedure calls for measured field. The current type decides what the field **can** find; verification proves what it **does**. Both choices belong in the inspection record - source, current type, and amperage are standard entries on every MT report.

figures.title: MT field sources - permanent magnet, yoke, bench unit

checkQuestions.question: The strongest case for a permanent magnet yoke is:
options: It produces the deepest field of any source
Its field strength is easy to adjust
It needs no power and cannot arc - useful in flammable atmospheres
It demagnetizes the part automatically when removed
checkQuestions.explanation: A permanent magnet needs no power supply and cannot spark, which suits tanks, no-power sites, and underwater work; the trade is zero field adjustment, mandatory lift checks, and difficult handling.

checkQuestions.question: AC concentrates its magnetic field at the part surface because of:
options: The skin effect from the constantly reversing current
The higher voltage AC equipment uses
Permeability dropping at depth
The particles dragging the field outward
checkQuestions.explanation: The rapid reversal of AC confines flux to the outer skin of the conductor - excellent for surface cracks and particle mobility, blind to subsurface flaws.

checkQuestions.question: Half-wave DC is the classic pairing with dry powder on rough welds because it provides:
options: A pure surface field with no penetration
Subsurface penetration plus pulsation that keeps particles mobile
The smoothest, steadiest field available
Automatic demagnetization between pulses
checkQuestions.explanation: HWDC's one-directional average penetrates like DC while its pulsing vibrates the powder almost like AC - depth and mobility in one current.

checkQuestions.question: Which current type gives the deepest field penetration?
options: AC at line frequency
Half-wave rectified DC
Full-wave rectified DC
All three penetrate equally
checkQuestions.explanation: Full-wave DC produces the steadiest one-directional field and fills the most cross-section, making it the choice for near-subsurface capability on bench units.

checkQuestions.question: Per common practice, the lifting-test values for yokes at the working pole spacing are about:
options: 10 lb for DC yokes, 40 lb for AC yokes
10 lb for AC yokes, 40 lb for DC and permanent magnet yokes
5 lb for all yokes
100 lb for all yokes
checkQuestions.explanation: Practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 use approximately a 10-pound lift for AC yokes and 40 pounds for DC and permanent magnet yokes, checked at the pole spacing to be used.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §6.1-6.2 (sources of magnetic fields)
ASTM E709 (yoke lifting checks and current types - general practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 7 (yoke and current technique - general practice)
